title: Your First Program 🚀
description: Write and understand your very first lines of Java code.

slides.title: Say Hello to the World 🌍
slides.content:
Every programmer's journey begins with making the computer print the phrase: **"Hello World"**.

In Java, to print something on the screen, we use a special command:

```java
System.out.println("Hello World");
```

Let's break this down.

slides.title: See It in Action ✨
slides.content:
Here is the exact code. Notice that the text we want to print is wrapped in double quotes `" "` and put inside the `( )`.

Also, the line ends with a semicolon `;`. This is like the period at the end of an English sentence!

quizData.question: Which of these perfectly prints Hello?
quizData.explanation: In Java, you need `System.out.println`, quotes around text, and a semicolon at the end!

slides.title: Wait, what's 'println'? 🤔
slides.content:
## Printing Magic

`println` stands for **Print Line**.

When we use `System.out.println("Hello");`, it prints "Hello" and then moves the invisible cursor to the **next line**.

slides.title: Printing Multiple Lines 📑
slides.content: Let's see what happens when we use `println` twice in a row.

slides.title: Just 'print' instead of 'println'? 🧐
slides.content:
You can also use just `System.out.print("...")`.

 unlike `println`, simple `print` **does NOT** move to the next line. Anything printed after it will appear right next to it, on the same line.

slides.title: No New Line Output ➡️
slides.content: Can you guess what this will output? It prints "Hello World" all bunched up together on one line!

quizData.question: What is the difference between print() and println()?

options.text: println() adds a new line at the end, print() does not.
print() prints text, println() prints numbers.

quizData.explanation: `println` prints the text and drops down to the next line, whereas `print` stays on the same line!

slides.title: Awesome! 🎉
slides.content: You now know the most important command in Java for giving output. Next, we are going to look at the invisible 'shell' that our `System.out.println();` commands have to live inside to actually work.
